elements.sent-2411-001: The Hayward regular BH solution attempted to resolve the curvature singularity issue by entering the domain of non-singular spacetimes.
elements.sent-2411-002: Recently, Dutta Roy and Kar (Phys.. Rev.
elements.sent-2411-003: D 106, 044028) expanded this solution to encompass a broader range of spacetimes.
elements.sent-2411-004: These spacetimes are constructed based on the Damour-Solodukhin prescription, which involves introducing different metric parameters in the $g_{tt}$ and $g_{rr}$ components of the original Hayward line element, and are characterized by two parameters ($\sigma, \kappa$).
elements.sent-2411-005: This generalization gives rise to both known and novel regular/singular BHs as well as various types of wormhole spacetimes.
elements.sent-2411-006: In this work, we explore the spacetimes that emerge for different values of ($\sigma, \kappa$) from the generalized Hayward metric, particularly focusing on their shadows in vacuum and when surrounded by plasma.
elements.sent-2411-007: Intriguingly, we observe the presence of both photon and anti-photon spheres for certain regular spacetimes.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our study highlights the differences in the shadows of different types of regular spacetime compared to those of the singular BH derived from the generalized Hayward metric and also sheds light on the impact of plasma on the shadow radius.
elements.sent-2411-009: The theory of general relativity (GR), with black holes (BH) as one of the solutions, is widely regarded as the most successful theory of gravity.
elements.sent-2411-010: Until recently, observational tests of GR were predominantly based on probing the weak field limit of gravity \cite{Hartle_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: The era of testing GR in the regime of strong gravity and hence the existence of BHs started with the discovery of gravitational waves \cite{LIGOScientific:2016aoc by the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA collaboration and simultaneously with the first detection of the shadow of the supermassive BH in M87 galaxy by the Event Horizon Telescope (EHT) collaboration \cite{EventHorizonTelescope:2019dse .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Despite the success of GR, it is plagued with problems: the existence of curvature singularity, issues with quantization of gravity, are a few among them.
elements.sent-2411-013: To tackle the issue of curvature singularity in GR, a possible solution exists in literature, that of non-singular spacetimes.
elements.sent-2411-014: These regular classes of solutions can be broadly divided into spacetimes with and without horizon like the regular BHs and wormholes, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-015: However, upon a closer inspection of these solutions, one realizes that the issue of singularity is traded for other issues, such as energy condition violating matter for wormholes \cite{morris_1988,thorne_1988 or mass-inflation instabilities in regular BHs \cite{ori_1991,poisson_1989,carballo_2018,carballo_2021 , provided that these solutions are viewed in the context of GR.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: While there exists multiple works which show the existence of wormholes in alternative theories of gravity without the need of `exotic' matter \cite{lobo_2008,varieschi_2015, kord_2015, ovgun_2019, zubair_2017, lobo_2009, boehmer_2012, shaikh_2016,kanti_2012,mehdizadeh_2015, maeda_2008, kanti_2011,shaikh_2015 and possible ways of taming the mass-inflation instability of regular BHs \cite{Carballo-Rubio:2022kad,bonanno_2021 , the issues still remains to be conclusively addressed.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: BHs are ubiquitous in physics, and at the same time they are the simplest objects in GR.
elements.sent-2411-018: For this reason they are often termed as the hydrogen atom of GR.
elements.sent-2411-019: The simplistic nature of the BHs, along with their extreme gravitational fields, make them the perfect testing bed to examine GR at strong field regimes.
elements.sent-2411-020: Towards this direction, the EHT collaboration has drawn much attention, and shifted the focus of the entire observational community with its first observations via the Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI) of the supermassive BHs at the centers of the galaxies M87 \cite{EventHorizonTelescope:2019dse and Sgr $A^*$ \cite{EventHorizonTelescope:2022wkp .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Interestingly, it is to be noted that the EHT observations have achieved the required frequency to resolve the horizon-scale emission from the vicinity of the supermassive BHs at the center of the galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-022: As a result, the observed light from these extreme compact objects includes photons whose trajectories near the horizon of the BHs are deviated from their actual travel paths and describes nearly bound spherical orbits around the BH.
elements.sent-2411-023: This humongous achievement was made possible by a global network of radio telescopes working in parallel to create a virtual earth-sized observatory.
elements.sent-2411-024: By capturing and analyzing the radio waves emitted by the surrounding accretion disk, the EHT has once again provided a confirmation of the theoretical predictions of photon paths within the theory of GR.
elements.sent-2411-025: Although GR has achieved significant successes, it also faces severe challenges on the theoretical front.
elements.sent-2411-026: One of the most problematic areas in GR is the presence of the singularities at the core of the BH spacetimes, which raises serious question about the credibility of the theory.
elements.sent-2411-027: The issue that GR is ultraviolet (UV)-incomplete is related to this fact.
elements.sent-2411-028: It is therefore proposed that GR requires modifications in the regions where the spacetime curvature becomes high.
elements.sent-2411-029: Such modifications is necessary if one requires a theory of gravity which is UV complete.
elements.sent-2411-030: One potential approach to address the singularity issue is to consider specific types of matter distributions, which will lead to singularity-free BH solutions.
elements.sent-2411-031: Bardeen first proposed the metric for such a nonsingular BH geometry \cite{bardeen_1968 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Later on, by coupling GR with nonlinear electrodynamics (NLED), a plethora of regular BH solutions with electric and magnetic charges were derived \cite{Ayon-Beato:1998hmi, Ayon-Beato:1999qin, Ayon-Beato:1999kuh, ayon-Beato_2000, ayon-Beato_2004, Dymnikova20044417, Bronnikov2001, Shankaranarayanan20041095 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Later on, Hayward proposed a static spherically symmetric BH solution in regular space-time \cite{hayward_2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: This metric describes an isolated regular four dimensional spherically symmetric spacetime and contains a length scale $\ell$, apart from which, there is only one parameter: the mass of the BH.
elements.sent-2411-035: At large distances the metric takes the Schwarzschild form, while at the origin it is regular and has de Sitter form.
elements.sent-2411-036: The distinguishing feature of such a metric is its scaling behaviour, i.e. the scaling transformation of the coordinates, parameters and the metric preserves the form of the metric.
elements.sent-2411-037: The literature related to shadows of different extreme compact objects including BHs is very rich and because of the present interest in EHT collaboration's result, there has been a flurry of works in this direction.
elements.sent-2411-038: Here, we briefly mention some of the important works towards the development of the subject.
elements.sent-2411-039: The shadow of the Schwarzschild black hole was first discussed in \cite{Synge:1966mon, Luminet:1979nyg .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Bardeen \cite{bardeen_1973 , again, was the first to describe the nature of the shadow cast by a rotating Kerr black hole, consequently it was generalised in the case of a Kerr-Newman BH in \cite{deVries:1999tiy .}}
elements.sent-2411-041: The shadow of the Kerr black hole as well as a Kerr naked singularity was discussed in \cite{PhysRevD.80.024042 by introducing two new observables.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Going beyond the standard GR solutions of Einstein's equations, the study of shadows in BHs in other theories had been discussed extensively in the literature, viz.
elements.sent-2411-043: Einstein-Maxwell-Dilaton-Axion black hole \cite{Shao-Wen_Wei_2013 , Kerr-Taub-NUT black hole \cite{Abdujabbarov:2012bn , shadows of static spherically symmetric and rotating Kerr BHs in modified gravity (MOG) theory \cite{Moffat:2015kva , rotating braneworld black hole \cite{PhysRevD.85.064019 , rotating non-Kerr type BHs \cite{PhysRevD.88.064004 , BHs in an expanding Universe \cite{Roy:2020dyy , BHs in dynamical Chern-Simons modified gravity \cite{Rodriguez:2024ijx to name a few.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: For more detailed on the methods and results in calculating shadows, we refer the readers to the existing reviews \cite{Cunha:2018acu,Perlick:2021aok,Lupsasca:2024wkp .}
elements.sent-2411-045: On the other hand, the study of shadows in regular BH backgrounds has gained considerable interests in recent years.
elements.sent-2411-046: There has been a lot of works done in this direction, see \cite{Li:2013jra,Abdujabbarov:2016hnw,Stuchlik:2019uvf,Dymnikova:2019vuz,GHOSH2020115088,Uniyal:2023ahv,KumarWalia:2024yxn for more details.}
elements.sent-2411-047: In our work, we take a pragmatic approach and address the observational aspects of a class of regular spacetime solutions called the generalized Hayward metric recently developed in Dutta Roy and Kar \cite{DuttaRoy:2022ytr , which describes wormholes, regular BH spacetimes along with the singular BH depending on the choice of the metric parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The generalized Hayward metric is a perfect testing ground for comparing the shadows of the regular spacetimes to that of the singular BHs.
elements.sent-2411-049: To our knowledge, the present study incorporating different classes of compact objects, eg.
elements.sent-2411-050: Even though studies on each different topics including wormhole shadows (see \cite{Kumar:2023wfp for details on wormhole shadows and for more references on the topic) have been performed individually, the present approach to study all these under the same umbrella is new.}
elements.sent-2411-051: It is known that certain regular spacetimes possess an anti-photon ring along with the usual photon ring.
elements.sent-2411-052: We explore in detail the occurrence of photon/anti-photon rings in all classes of geometries originating from the generalized Hayward metric and probe the capability of the shadow observations to distinguish the regular spacetimes from the singular BH.
elements.sent-2411-053: It is known that the shadow of a compact object is associated with the photons scattered from the innermost circular null orbits and, hence, is a characteristic of the compact object.
elements.sent-2411-054: However, this holds true assuming the trajectory of these photons are unaffected throughout their journey from the point of emission to the typical asymptotic observer.
elements.sent-2411-055: In a realistic scenario, compact objects are always surrounded by an interstellar medium, which has a non-trivial effect on the photon trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-056: Hence it is natural to incorporate the effects of the environment in the computations of shadow.
elements.sent-2411-057: As a first step to address these issues, we analytically handle the computations of shadow in the presence of a plasma surrounding the compact object.
elements.sent-2411-058: Although our analysis corresponds to a simplified scenario which are different from a real astrophysical environment, these analytic calculations allow us to understand the explicit dependencies of the shadow profile on the metric and plasma parameters in contrast to numerical simulations.
elements.sent-2411-059: To this extent, we follow the techniques described in \cite{Perlick:2015prd and restrict to non-magnetized cold plasma consisting of two fluids, which are modeled by ions and electrons.}
elements.sent-2411-060: The Hamiltonian for the photon trajectories in the presence of plasma in a curved spacetime background has been done at various levels of sophistication.
elements.sent-2411-061: For example, the rigorous derivation of Hamiltonian for magnetized plasma was achieved by Breur and Ehlers in \cite{Breuer:1980proc,Breuer:1981proc .}
elements.sent-2411-062: For a much simpler case of non-magnetized plasma fluids that we are interested in, the same analysis was carried out in \cite{Perlick:2000book,Synge:1960book .}
elements.sent-2411-063: These results were then applied to the photon trajectors in the equatorial plane of Kerr spacetime by Perlick and others in \cite{Perlick:2000book,Bisnovatyi:2008grav,Bisnovatyi:2010mon,Tsupko:2013prd and in a general setup in \cite{Morozova:2013 .}}
elements.sent-2411-064: The rest of the paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-065: In section II, we briefly discuss the generalized Hayward metric and the spacetimes arising for different metric parameter values.
elements.sent-2411-066: Section III summarizes the computation of the shadow cast by a static spherically symmetric solution.
elements.sent-2411-067: The main results are discussed in Section IV, consisting of the shadow, with and without plasma, for the different spacetimes originating from the generalized Hayward metric.
elements.sent-2411-068: We summarise the conclusions in Section V.
elements.sent-2411-069: Unless otherwise stated we have used geometrised units $G = c = 1$.
elements.section-2411-002: Review of spacetimes originating from generalised Hayward metric
elements.sent-2411-070: In this section, we will discuss the structure of the generalized Hayward metric and give a brief recap about the different spacetimes that originate for different parameter values.. \begin{spreadlines {-0.4em.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: It is important to note that for $M_1 =M$, this metric reduces to the Hayward regular BH, with the square of the parameter $\ell$ being identified with the inverse of the cosmological constant in the small $r$ limit.
elements.sent-2411-072: In fact, the construction of the generalized Hayward metric is motivated from the Hayward BH case by considering two different mass parameters in the $g_{tt}$ and $g_{rr}$ components.
elements.sent-2411-073: The incorporation of a different mass parameter \( M_1 \) in the \( g_{tt \) component of the metric, such that \( M_1 \neq M \), is inspired by the Damour-Solodukhin wormhole construction based on the Schwarzschild metric \cite{damour_2007 .}}
elements.sent-2411-074: By introducing a different mass parameter into the metric of the Schwarzschild BH, the well-studied Damour-Solodukhin wormhole is obtained, while a procedure on the same vein on the Hayward regular BH resulted in the generalized Hayward metric as shown in Eq.(\ref{eq:metric ).}
elements.sent-2411-075: Following \cite{DuttaRoy:2022ytr , we re-write Eq.(\ref{eq:metric ) as, \begin{eqnarray}ds^2 = - \Big( 1- \frac{2 \sigma x^2}{x^3+ 2 \sigma \kappa^2}\Big) dt^2 + \frac{dr^2}{\Big( 1- \frac{2 x^2}{x^3+ 2 \kappa^2}\Big)} + r^2 (d\theta^2 + \sin^2 \theta d\phi^2),\label{eq:dimensionless_metric}\end{eqnarray} where the parameters $M,M_1,\ell$ are written in a dimensionless form using $\sigma=\frac{M_1 {M}$, $\kappa=\frac{\ell {M}$ with the radial coordinate $x=\frac{r {M}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: We will use the metric of the form given in Eq.(\ref{eq:dimensionless_metric ) for rest of the discussions.}
elements.sent-2411-077: For different values of $(\sigma,\kappa)$, the spacetimes are classified into wormholes/BHs depending on the presence of throat/event horizon.
elements.sent-2411-078: Fig.(\ref{fig:parameter_space ) shows the six different classes of spacetimes corresponding to different values of $(\sigma,\kappa)$ whose properties are summarized in Table \ref{tab:classification .}}

The only spacetime with singularity is the Schwarzschild BH while all others are regular.} \label{tab:classification}

elements.sent-2411-097: In reality, astrophysical objects are surrounded by an interstellar medium, generally considered to be plasma in the near horizon region.

elements.sent-2411-272: The `shadow' in the context of BHs and other astrophysical compact objects is defined as a dark silhouette against a bright background which is formed due to the gravitational bending of light.

elements.sent-2411-324: In this section, we contextualize these results using observational data from EHT to determine if any of these spacetimes are viable candidates to represent an astrophysical compact object.
elements.sent-2411-325: Specifically, we consider the most recent EHT estimate for the angular diameter ($\Phi$) of the shadow of Sgr $\rm A^*$, given by $\Phi = 48.7 \pm 7.0\ \mu as$ \cite{EventHorizonTelescope:2022wkp and study if any of these spacetimes can represent this object.}
elements.sent-2411-326: This analysis incorporates priors on the distance to Sgr $\rm A^*$ and estimated BH mass which are obtained from: Keck and the Very Large Telescope Interferometer (VLTI) \cite{GRAVITY-1,Gravity-2 .}
elements.sent-2411-327: The Keck team reported a distance of $r_o = 7959 \pm 59 \pm 32\; pc$ and a BH mass of $M = (3.975 \pm 0.058 \pm 0.026) \times 10^6 M_{\odot}$ \cite{Keck:2019txf .}
elements.sent-2411-328: In contrast, VLTI, in collaboration with GRAVITY estimates the distance to be $r_o = 8277 \pm 9 \pm 33\; pc$ and mass $M = (4.297 \pm 0.012 \pm 0.040) \times 10^6 M_{\odot}$ \cite{GRAVITY-1, Gravity-2 .}
elements.sent-2411-329: In order to relate these results to our framework, we need to convert the angular shadow diameter to shadow radius.
elements.sent-2411-330: This is achieved by first relating the angular diameter of the shadow with shadow radius which is given by \begin{equation}\frac{r_{\text{sh}}}{r_o} = \tan\left( \frac{1}{2} \Phi \right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-331: Note that the distance $r_o$ used above is obtained from the priors considered for generating the angular diameter of the shadow.
elements.sent-2411-332: Also, the shadow radius obtained above is not dimensionless, it can be converted to a dimensionless quantity by normalizing it with appropriate factors of BH mass (from the mass prior), speed of light and the gravitational constant.
elements.sent-2411-333: Carrying out this computations using the VLTI and Gravity collaboration data we obtain the following constraint on dimensionless shadow radius \begin{equation}4.35548 \leq x_{\text{sh}} \leq 5.81695.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-334: In principle, the same analysis can be done with the Keck data, which would lead to marginally different bounds.
elements.sent-2411-335: However, the overall conclusions remain largely unaffected.

elements.sent-2411-368: To summarise, the Schwarzschild BH remains consistent with EHT data in both vacuum and plasma scenarios, though only for small values of the plasma parameter.
elements.sent-2411-369: In retrospect, this is expected given the small deviation parameter as mentioned in EHT results \cite{EventHorizonTelescope:2022wkp .}
elements.sent-2411-370: In contrast, the Schwarzschild wormhole is ruled out entirely, as its shadow radius lies below the observational lower bound and decreases further in the presence of plasma.
elements.sent-2411-371: Among wormhole spacetimes, the Damour-Solodukhin wormhole remains a viable solution.
elements.sent-2411-372: In homogeneous plasma, both single- and double-peak potential configurations can be accommodated, while in the non-homogeneous case, only double-peak configurations remain possible.
elements.sent-2411-373: A similar trend is observed for the Hayward-Damour-Solodukhin wormhole, where multi-peak potentials are favored at lower plasma parameter values, and single-peak solutions are excluded in the presence of non-homogeneous plasma.
elements.sent-2411-374: The Hayward wormhole is not supported by the EHT observations, as its shadow radius consistently falls below the allowed range regardless of plasma presence.
elements.sent-2411-375: On the other hand, the regular Hayward BH remains compatible with observations, although the inclusion of plasma significantly restricts the viable range of parameters.. \FloatBarrier.
elements.sent-2411-376: In this work, we have performed a detailed investigation of the shadows cast by various spacetime geometries arising from different parameter regimes of the generalized Hayward metric~\cite{DuttaRoy:2022ytr .}
elements.sent-2411-377: The metric in Eq.\eqref{eq:dimensionless_metric depends on parameters $(\sigma,\kappa)$ and is obtained by introducing distinct mass parameters in the $g_{tt}$ and $g_{rr}$ components of the original Hayward regular black hole metric.}
elements.sent-2411-378: This metric gives rise to four distinct kinds of wormholes, along with a regular and a singular BH solution as illustrated in Fig.(\ref{fig:parameter_space ) and summarized in Table \ref{tab:classification .}}
elements.sent-2411-379: While the spacetime properties and quasinormal mode spectra of these solutions have been analyzed in~\cite{DuttaRoy:2022ytr , here we focus on their shadow characteristics.}
elements.sent-2411-380: We have studied the dependence of shadow radius on the metric parameters for all spacetimes and plotted their deviation from the Schwarzschild BH shadow, corresponding to $(\sigma = 1, \kappa = 0)$.
elements.sent-2411-381: This analysis is especially relevant in the context of black hole mimickers, where particular parameter choices yield non-singular spacetimes whose shadow radius is nearly indistinguishable from that of a Schwarzschild BH.
elements.sent-2411-382: For instance, we observe that the Hayward regular BH yields a shadow radius nearly identical to Schwarzschild's for small $\kappa$ values (see Fig.~\ref{fig:HBH_shadow ).}
elements.sent-2411-383: Similarly, the Damour-Solodukhin wormhole approaches the Schwarzschild shadow as $\sigma \rightarrow 1$ (Fig.~\ref{fig:DSWH_shadow ).}
elements.sent-2411-384: However, significant deviations appear as $\kappa$ increases or $\sigma$ decreases, reflecting the growing difference in the underlying geometry from the Schwarzschild case.
elements.sent-2411-385: We also observe a notable feature in the most general subclass the Hayward-Damour-Solodukhin (HDS) wormhole where, for certain values of $(\sigma, \kappa)$, the effective potential admits two or three photon spheres.
elements.sent-2411-386: Depending on the metric parameters, this potential can exhibit single, double, or even triple peaks.
elements.sent-2411-387: We find that the shadow radius of the HDS wormhole increases with $\sigma$ (for fixed $\kappa$) and decreases with increasing $\kappa$ (for fixed $\sigma$).
elements.sent-2411-388: In contrast, all remaining wormhole and BH solutions possess a single photon sphere.
elements.sent-2411-389: The above conclusions pertain to vacuum spacetimes, where no surrounding medium influences the light propagation.
elements.sent-2411-390: However, realistic astrophysical BHs exist within matter-rich environments.
elements.sent-2411-391: As a first step towards incorporating such effects, we considered the impact of plasma on the shadow structure.
elements.sent-2411-392: For analytical simplicity we focused on two plasma density profiles: a homogeneous plasma $\Omega(x) = k_0$ and a non-homogeneous profile $\Omega(x) = \frac{k_x {x}$.}
elements.sent-2411-393: We computed the shadow radii for all classes of spacetimes under these conditions and compared the results to the vacuum case.
elements.sent-2411-394: A consolidated summary of the shadow's dependence on both the metric and plasma parameters is provided in Table~\ref{tab:summary .}
elements.sent-2411-395: Finally, we compare our results with the observational constraints from the EHT image of the shadow of Sgr $\rm A^*$.
elements.sent-2411-396: Upon imposing these constraints, regular black hole solutions do not see any significant change in the allowed parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-397: We also find that for wormhole solutions, the allowed parameter ranges for the spacetimes under consideration become significantly restricted.
elements.sent-2411-398: Our analysis indicates that a subclass of wormhole solutions exhibiting multi-peak effective potentials—particularly in the presence of plasma media—are more consistent with the observational data than those characterized by single-peak potentials.
elements.sent-2411-399: Interestingly, this finding stands in contrast to the conclusions drawn from quasinormal mode analyses, where single-barrier effective potentials were preferred as viable black hole mimickers \cite{DuttaRoy:2022ytr .}
elements.sent-2411-400: If the shadow-based analysis is accurate, this tension implies that compact objects resembling wormholes might exhibit characteristic late-time echoes in their gravitational wave signals offering a complementary observational signature to be explored in future detections.Current gravitational wave detectors have limited sensitivity when it comes to detecting the ringdown from the merger of compact objects and possible echoes if present.
elements.sent-2411-401: Thus the possibility of detecting echoes through improved future gravitational wave detectors keeps the wormhole geometries characterised with multi-peak potentials a viable spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-402: Regular BH solutions also remain viable, though with a significantly narrow choice of homogeneous plasma parameter when plasma is introduced.
elements.sent-2411-403: This makes it difficult to distinguish them observationally from standard GR black holes.
elements.sent-2411-404: Future high-resolution observations, both through shadows and gravitational waves, may enhance this discriminatory power, potentially uncovering subtle features unique to exotic compact objects.
elements.sent-2411-405: It is important to note that more realistic astrophysical scenarios involve additional complexities such as magnetic fields, accretion flows, and nontrivial plasma distributions, which cannot be treated analytically and need to be addressed.
elements.sent-2411-406: However, addressing these aspects will require a fully numerical approach and is beyond the scope of the present work.
elements.sent-2411-407: Nonetheless, the generalized Hayward metric offers a versatile framework for systematically studying the shadow characteristics of a wide spectrum of regular and singular compact objects.
